1. Scrapers (and anything Carleton-specific)\\\\n\\\\n- **Main entry point**\\\\n  - **File**: &#96;/Users/katespurr/Desktop/Halifax Now/Scrapers/general scraper/main.py&#96;\\\\n  - **Role**: CLI runner that selects a single site scraper and writes a CSV via &#96;write_csv&#96;.\\\\n  - **Registered scrapers**:\\\\n    - &#96;discoverhalifax&#96; → &#96;DiscoverHalifaxScraper()&#96; from &#96;/Users/katespurr/Desktop/Halifax Now/Scrapers/general scraper/scrapers/discoverhalifax.py&#96;\\\\n    - &#96;jumpcomedy&#96; → &#96;JumpComedyScraper()&#96; from &#96;/Users/katespurr/Desktop/Halifax Now/Scrapers/general scraper/scrapers/jumpcomedy.py&#96;\\\\n    - &#96;hfxcomedyfest&#96; → &#96;HfxComedyFestScraper()&#96; from &#96;/Users/katespurr/Desktop/Halifax Now/Scrapers/general scraper/scrapers/hfxcomedyfest.py&#96;\\\\n\\\\n- **Carleton-specific code**\\\\n  - I found **no files, classes, or strings mentioning &#96;carleton&#96;** anywhere in this repo, other than in SpecStory history markdown used as AI logs.\\\\n  - So in this project there is **no dedicated “The Carleton” scraper**; The Carleton events you’re seeing on the site must be coming from:\\\\n    - another repo/pipeline (e.g. your &#96;halifax_event_scrapers_v3&#96; master pipeline), or\\\\n    - an upstream source like Discover Halifax (if they list Carleton events and you eventually scrape/merge that elsewhere).\\\\n\\\\n### 2. Shared event model / data structure\\\\n\\\\n- **Event representation**\\\\n  - **File**: &#96;/Users/katespurr/Desktop/Halifax Now/Scrapers/general scraper/utils/helpers.py&#96;\\\\n  - **Structure**: events are plain **Python dicts** whose keys come from &#96;CSV_COLUMNS&#96;:\\\\n    - &#96;\\\\\\\"Event Name\\\\\\\"&#96;, &#96;\\\\\\\"Event Description\\\\\\\"&#96;, &#96;\\\\\\\"Venue Name\\\\\\\"&#96;, &#96;\\\\\\\"Venue Address\\\\\\\"&#96;,\\\\n      &#96;\\\\\\\"Event Start Date\\\\\\\"&#96;, &#96;\\\\\\\"Event Start Time\\\\\\\"&#96;, &#96;\\\\\\\"Event End Date\\\\\\\"&#96;, &#96;\\\\\\\"Event End Time\\\\\\\"&#96;,\\\\n      &#96;\\\\\\\"All Day Event\\\\\\\"&#96;, &#96;\\\\\\\"Event Timezone\\\\\\\"&#96;, &#96;\\\\\\\"Event Category\\\\\\\"&#96;, &#96;\\\\\\\"Event Tags\\\\\\\"&#96;,\\\\n      &#96;\\\\\\\"Event Website\\\\\\\"&#96;, &#96;\\\\\\\"Event Cost\\\\\\\"&#96;, &#96;\\\\\\\"Featured Image\\\\\\\"&#96;, &#96;\\\\\\\"Show Map\\\\\\\"&#96;, &#96;\\\\\\\"Show Map Link\\\\\\\"&#96;.\\\\n  - &#96;write_csv(events, output_path)&#96; just writes these dicts as rows; there is **no &#96;Event&#96;/&#96;Show&#96; class, no IDs, no explicit uniqueness fields** in this repo.\\\\n\\\\n- **Per-scraper outputs**\\\\n  - Each scraper’s &#96;parse_event&#96; returns **one such dict**, e.g. in:\\\\n    - &#96;/Users/katespurr/Desktop/Halifax Now/Scrapers/general scraper/scrapers/discoverhalifax.py&#96;\\\\n    - &#96;/Users/katespurr/Desktop/Halifax Now/Scrapers/general scraper/scrapers/jumpcomedy.py&#96;\\\\n    - &#96;/Users/katespurr/Desktop/Halifax Now/Scrapers/general scraper/scrapers/hfxcomedyfest.py&#96;\\\\n\\\\n### 3. Existing deduplication / upsert logic\\\\n\\\\nWithin this repo, there is **no DB, no upsert logic, and no cross-scraper dedup**. The only actual deduplication I found is:\\\\n\\\\n- **URL-level dedup in the Hfx Comedy Fest scraper**\\\\n  - **File**: &#96;/Users/katespurr/Desktop/Halifax Now/Scrapers/general scraper/scrapers/hfxcomedyfest.py&#96;\\\\n  - **Function**: &#96;HfxComedyFestScraper.get_event_links&#96;\\\\n  - **Logic**:\\\\n    - It uses Playwright to extract all links that match the Tixr event URL pattern.\\\\n    - Then deduplicates them **purely by URL string**, preserving order:\\\\n      - &#96;links = list(dict.fromkeys(all_links))&#96;\\\\n  - This prevents scraping the **same Tixr event URL multiple times** if the page lists duplicate &#96;\u003ca\u003e&#96; tags, but:\\\\n    - It does **not** deduplicate events across runs.\\\\n    - It does **not** deduplicate by title/date/time.\\\\n    - It does **not** touch how events are written to the CSV.\\\\n\\\\nEverywhere else:\\\\n\\\\n- &#96;DiscoverHalifaxScraper&#96; and &#96;JumpComedyScraper&#96;:\\\\n  - Collect lists of URLs, do **no dedup** beyond whatever their code for building the list implicitly does (currently just appending every matching link).\\\\n  - &#96;parse_event&#96; builds a dict and returns it; &#96;main.py&#96; collects them into &#96;events&#96; and writes them straight out to CSV.\\\\n- There is **no pipeline here that merges outputs from multiple scrapers**, no &#96;merge_*&#96; or &#96;compare_*&#96; scripts, and no code that talks to your site or a database.\\\\n\\\\nAny “upsert vs insert” behaviour you see on the website is therefore coming from:\\\\n\\\\n- **Your WordPress / The Events Calendar importer**, or\\\\n- **Your separate master pipeline repo** (referenced in older SpecStory notes as things like &#96;merge_master_events.py&#96; / &#96;compare_site_xml_to_master.py&#96; in another project path), not from this &#96;general scraper&#96; project.\\\\n\\\\n### 4. How events are currently keyed for uniqueness (in this repo)\\\\n\\\\n- **In this repo alone**:\\\\n  - **Events are not given an explicit uniqueness key** (no &#96;id&#96;, no slug, no hash).\\\\n  - The only place uniqueness is enforced is **URL-level** in &#96;HfxComedyFestScraper.get_event_links&#96;, where an event is considered unique by its **Tixr event URL string**.\\\\n  - When writing CSV, &#96;write_csv&#96; simply dumps every event dict it receives; it does **no dedup**.\\\\n\\\\n- **Implication for The Carleton**\\\\n  - Because this project just emits flat CSVs with one row per scraped event and **no dedup logic by title/date/time**, any de-duplication or “same event” detection for The Carleton (e.g., when titles change because of low ticket sales) is **not happening here**.\\\\n  - That identity logic must be:\\\\n    - in your **downstream master pipeline** (e.g. where you create &#96;master_events.csv&#96; and compare to site events), or\\\\n    - in your **WordPress/TEC import settings** (how they treat “same event” based on title + start date/time + venue, etc.).\\\\n\\\\nIf you’d like next, I can scan your master pipeline repo (e.g. &#96;halifax_event_scrapers_v3&#96;) to pinpoint exactly where The Carleton events are keyed for uniqueness (likely in a merge/dedup script and/or the compare-to-site logic).\\\"}\"},\"status\":\"completed\",\"context\":{\"composers\":[],\"selectedCommits\":[],\"selectedPullRequests\":[],\"selectedImages\":[],\"folderSelections\":[],\"fileSelections\":[],\"selections\":[],\"terminalSelections\":[],\"selectedDocs\":[],\"externalLinks\":[],\"cursorRules\":[],\"cursorCommands\":[],\"gitPRDiffSelections\":[],\"subagentSelections\":[],\"browserSelections\":[],\"mentions\":{\"composers\":{},\"selectedCommits\":{},\"selectedPullRequests\":{},\"gitDiff\":[],\"gitDiffFromBranchToMain\":[],\"selectedImages\":{},\"folderSelections\":{},\"fileSelections\":{},\"terminalFiles\":{},\"selections\":{},\"terminalSelections\":{},\"selectedDocs\":{},\"externalLinks\":{},\"diffHistory\":[],\"cursorRules\":{},\"cursorCommands\":{},\"uiElementSelections\":[],\"consoleLogs\":[],\"ideEditorsState\":[],\"gitPRDiffSelections\":{},\"subagentSelections\":{},\"browserSelections\":{}}},\"generatingBubbleIds\":[],\"isReadingLongFile\":false,\"codeBlockData\":{},\"originalFileStates\":{},\"newlyCreatedFiles\":[],\"newlyCreatedFolders\":[],\"createdAt\":1772630206068,\"hasChangedContext\":false,\"activeTabsShouldBeReactive\":true,\"capabilities\":[{\"type\":30,\"data\":{}},{\"type\":15,\"data\":{\"bubbleDataMap\":\"{}\"}},{\"type\":29,\"data\":{}}],\"name\":\"Event Parameters:

```json
{
  "name": "carleton-event-dedup",
  "overview": "Adjust The Carleton event pipeline so that repeated scrapes update an existing event instead of creating a new one when only the title changes, by basing identity on a stable key (like URL or platform ID) rather than the raw title.",
  "plan": "## Goal\n\nMake The Carleton events **idempotent across scrapes** so that when the venue tweaks titles (e.g., to signal low ticket sales), the site **updates the existing event instead of creating a duplicate**.\n\n## High-level approach\n\n- **Identify where Carleton events are keyed for uniqueness** in your dedicated Carleton scraper repo and downstream merge/import pipeline.\n- **Introduce a stable event identity field** (e.g., derived from event URL or ticketing platform ID) that does not change when the title changes.\n- **Update dedup/upsert logic** so that it matches events primarily on this stable key rather than on the title.\n- **Optionally normalize titles** (strip common marketing suffixes like \"(Low Tickets)\" or \"New Show Added\") as a fallback, to reduce accidental duplicates even when no explicit ID is available.\n- **Test with before/after snapshots** to verify that a title change results in an update, not a new row/post.\n\n## Context diagram\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;mermaid\nflowchart LR\n  carletonSite[\"Carleton Site\"] --\u003e carletonScraper[\"CarletonScraper\"]\n  carletonScraper --\u003e masterCsv[\"master_events.csv / DB\"]\n  masterCsv --\u003e importer[\"SiteImporter / WordPress\"]\n  importer --\u003e halifaxNow[\"HalifaxNow Site\"]\n\n  style masterCsv stroke-width:2px\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n- The **key change** happens between &#96;CarletonScraper&#96; and &#96;master_events.csv&#96; (add a stable ID) and in the logic that converts &#96;master_events.csv&#96; into site posts (use that ID for upserts).\n\n## Detailed steps\n\n### 1. Locate the Carleton scraper and downstream merge/dedup code\n\n- **Find the Carleton scraper module** in your other repo (e.g., something like &#96;[scrapers/carleton.py](scrapers/carleton.py)&#96; or similar).\n- **Identify the function that returns event dicts/rows**, analogous to &#96;parse_event&#96; in this &#96;general scraper&#96; project.\n- **Find the merge/dedup layer** that:\n  - Combines Carleton events into your master events dataset (e.g., &#96;[merge_master_events.py](merge_master_events.py)&#96;), and/or\n  - Compares master events to what is already on the site before import (e.g., &#96;[compare_site_xml_to_master.py](compare_site_xml_to_master.py)&#96; or a WordPress import script).\n- Confirm what fields are currently used as the **uniqueness key** (very likely something like &#96;Event Name + Event Start Date + Venue&#96; or just the title).\n\n### 2. Design a stable event identity for Carleton\n\n- **Preferred key: event URL or platform ID**\n  - If each Carleton event is hosted at a stable URL, use that URL (or a normalized form of it) as the event's **primary external ID**.\n  - If Carleton events are sold via a platform (e.g., Tixr, TicketPro) that exposes a numeric or slug ID, extract that and use it as the key.\n- **Fallback key (if URLs/IDs can change)**\n  - Use a composite key such as normalized:\n    - &#96;venue_name&#96; (\"The Carleton\"),\n    - &#96;event_start_date&#96; and &#96;event_start_time&#96;,\n    - plus a **heavily normalized title** (lowercased, punctuation-stripped, marketing suffixes removed).\n  - Example of a key builder in pseudocode:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;python\nkey = f\"carleton::{date}::{time}::{normalized_title}\"\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n- Decide on a **single canonical field name**, e.g. &#96;source_event_id&#96; or &#96;external_id&#96;, and use it consistently.\n\n### 3. Update the Carleton scraper to emit the stable ID\n\n- In the Carleton scraper's event-building function (e.g., &#96;parse_event&#96; or &#96;build_event_dict&#96; in &#96;[scrapers/carleton.py](scrapers/carleton.py)&#96;):\n  - **Extract the event URL** (already likely mapped to &#96;\"Event Website\"&#96;).\n  - Implement a helper like &#96;build_carleton_event_id(event_url, date, time, raw_title)&#96; that:\n    - Preferentially derives an ID from the **URL or embedded platform ID**.\n    - Falls back to a **normalized composite key** when necessary.\n  - **Add this ID** into the event dict under a new column/key such as &#96;\"Source Event ID\"&#96; (or another agreed name) so it flows through to your master CSV.\n\n- Keep the existing &#96;\"Event Name\"&#96; as-is so the marketing-friendly version still appears on the site; the new field is purely for identity.\n\n### 4. Change downstream dedup/upsert logic to use the stable ID\n\n- In your master merge script or database layer (e.g., &#96;[merge_master_events.py](merge_master_events.py)&#96;):\n  - When loading Carleton events, treat &#96;\"Source Event ID\"&#96; as the **primary key** for Carleton records.\n  - For each new scrape row:\n    - If a row with the same &#96;\"Source Event ID\"&#96; already exists, **update its mutable fields** (title, description, tags, image, etc.).\n    - If no row exists with that ID, **insert a new event**.\n\n- In your site import logic (e.g., WordPress / The Events Calendar importer):\n  - Wherever you currently match existing posts to incoming data, switch the lookup for Carleton events to use &#96;\"Source Event ID\"&#96; (e.g., stored in a custom field/meta or used to build a deterministic slug), instead of the raw title.\n  - Ensure that **title changes do not affect the lookup key**; the ID should remain unchanged as long as it's the same underlying event.\n\n### 5. Optional: Title normalization as a safety net\n\n- Add a title-normalization helper in the Carleton scraper or merge layer that:\n  - Lowercases and trims whitespace.\n  - Strips punctuation, multiple spaces, and common suffixes/prefixes like:\n    - \"- Low Tickets\", \"(Low Tickets)\", \"- New Date Added\", \"(Second Show Added)\", etc.\n- Use this normalized title:\n  - As part of the fallback key if you can't rely fully on URL/ID.\n  - To compare for **near-duplicates** when you have no &#96;Source Event ID&#96; (e.g., older historical rows).\n\n### 6. Testing and verification\n\n- **Create a small test dataset** for a single Carleton event:\n  - Run the scraper once and capture the output row where the event title is, say, \"Artist Name\".\n  - Manually edit the source page title (or mock HTML) to \"Artist Name - Low Tickets!\" and run the scraper again.\n- In your merge/import scripts, simulate or run the full pipeline and confirm:\n  - Both rows produce the **same &#96;Source Event ID&#96;**.\n  - The second run **updates** the existing event rather than creating a new one.\n  - No new duplicate appears on the site.\n- Optionally run a **backfill dedup** pass on existing Carleton events:\n  - Build IDs for historical rows using the same logic.\n  - Group by &#96;Source Event ID&#96; and collapse duplicates, keeping the most recent data.\n\n## Todos\n\n- **locate-carleton-scraper**: Find the dedicated Carleton scraper file and the associated merge/import scripts in your other repo, and confirm how uniqueness is currently determined.\n- **design-stable-id**: Decide on the exact &#96;Source Event ID&#96;/&#96;external_id&#96; format for Carleton events (URL-based, platform ID, or composite key) and document it.\n- **emit-id-in-scraper**: Update the Carleton scraper to compute and emit the stable ID field in each event dict/row.\n- **update-dedup-logic**: Modify the master merge/import logic to use the stable ID as the primary key for Carleton events and to update instead of insert when it matches.\n- **add-title-normalization**: Implement optional title-normalization and near-duplicate checks for Carleton as a fallback/safety net.\n- **test-with-title-changes**: Run before/after tests where the same event's title is changed on The Carleton site and verify no duplicates are created on your site.",
```

### Answer

Yes – I’ve now adjusted the logic so Carleton’s “low tickets” title tweaks won’t create duplicate events.

- **Stable Carleton event ID**: The Carleton scraper (`the_carleton_scraper.py`) now adds a `SOURCE EVENT ID` column, built from a normalized event URL (e.g. `carleton::https://www.thecarleton.ca/events/alfie-zappacosta-3`), so the same underlying event keeps the same ID even if the title changes.
- **Merge & archive dedupe by ID**: The master merge script (`merge_master_events.py`) now:
  - Uses that `Source Event ID` as the primary dedupe key for `carleton`/`thecarleton` rows, and
  - Uses an ID‑based archive key for Carleton, so future scrapes update the existing event instead of treating it as a brand‑new one.
- **Title normalization for “low tickets” variants**: Both the cross‑scraper name matcher (`normalize_event_name` in `merge_master_events.py`) and the site-vs-master comparer (`normalize_title` in `compare_site_xml_to_master_v2.py`) now strip phrases like “Low Tickets”, “Low Ticket Alert”, and “New Show Added”, so minor marketing changes in the title no longer cause mismatches or duplicate imports.
